sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presents with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in bruising, swelling, and instability on the outside of the ankle.
He reports difficulty walking due to pain and instability.
The patient had been working out regularly and was anxious to avoid disrupting his routine.
He also mentioned volunteering to coach his son's basketball team starting in April.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The ankle is swollen, with exquisite tenderness to palpation on the lateral side and some tenderness over the medial deltoid region.
The skin is intact, and capillary refill is brisk.
There is no malalignment.
Test Results: X-ray results show a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture, which is likely to require surgery to stabilize and promote healing.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; Suspected instability of the ankle; Ankle pain.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam to reduce swelling and pain.
I advised the patient to ice the ankle and keep it elevated regularly, especially at work since he works from home.
I also recommended the use of crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle.
I informed the patient that he may need to sit out for about three months, but he can continue coaching his son's basketball team in the meantime.
I will follow up with the patient to assess his progress and plan for surgery.
Follow-up: The patient will return to the office in three months for a follow-up appointment to assess his progress and plan for surgery.
